title: JavaScript Concepts
response:
Hoisting is JavaScript's behaviour of moving declarations to the top of their scope before code executes (conceptually — the engine does this during the compilation phase).

• Function declarations are fully hoisted — you can call them before they appear in the file.
• var declarations are hoisted but initialised to undefined — you can reference them before declaration but get undefined.
• let and const are hoisted but NOT initialised — accessing them before declaration throws a ReferenceError. This gap is called the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ).
• Function expressions (const fn = function(){}) follow the rules of their keyword (let/const), so they are NOT callable before the line of assignment.
